title: Apply: How Women Won the Vote
description: Apply your understanding of the steps activists took to secure women the right to vote with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Elections and Voting Rights: From the Founding through the Civil Rights Movement
Apply: How Women Won the Vote

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**What statement best summarizes the message in the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A woman holding a torch in the air walks across a map of the United States. She wears a dress that says "Votes for Women" the train. She walks from the western states towards the eastern states. The wester states are white. The eastern states are black. In the eastern states, women are reaching toward to the woman with the torch.
options.caption: “The Awakening” by Henry Mayer, 1915

choices.content: The abolition of slavery started in the west and moved east as more states agreed to ratify the Reconstruction Amendments.
The women’s suffrage movement secured the right to vote in the west and moved east to encourage more states to grant women the right to vote.
The eastern states opposed the ratification of an amendment that would extend citizenship to women.
The western states were the last states to extend women the right to vote and were the focus of the suffrage campaign in the late 1910s.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**The political cartoon most closely relates to which strategy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A woman holding a torch in the air walks across a map of the United States. She wears a dress that says "Votes for Women" the train. She walks from the western states towards the eastern states. The wester states are white. The eastern states are black. In the eastern states, women are reaching toward to the woman with the torch.
options.caption: “The Awakening” by Henry Mayer, 1915

choices.content: Securing women the right to vote at the state level to help bring about change at the national level
Engaging in civil disobedience to protest the widespread use of Jim Crow laws in the South
Petitioning state governments to support the Louisiana Purchase in order to add more states to the Unions
Suing state governments in the hopes that the Supreme Court would grant women the right to vote

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Maria F. Chandler’s 1866 letter to Thaddeus Stevens, a Radical Republican congressman.**

For years has Woman stood beside the ballot-box in her own native grace, dignity, and modesty, ballot in hand—hopefully awaiting that recognition of her political equality, guaranteed by a Republican form of Government. 

Why is the [[☃ definition 1]] denied us? Will any sane person declare that we are “Not of Sound mind”? . . . 

[H]ow extremely absurd it appears in the light of reason, to allow men the Elective Franchise who can neither read, nor write, and withhold it from women of cultivated minds and scholarly [[☃ definition 2]]. 

**Which statement best summarizes the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Women should not receive the right to vote before Black men, as the problems caused by slavery have not been resolved.
Women deserve the right to vote, as their husbands cannot represent their political beliefs properly.
Women deserve the right to vote more than less educated men do.
Women have had the right to vote for decades, but obstacles have made it impossible for them to exercise their right.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Maria F. Chandler’s 1866 letter to Thaddeus Stevens, a Radical Republican congressman.**

For years has Woman stood beside the ballot-box in her own native grace, dignity, and modesty, ballot in hand—hopefully awaiting that recognition of her political equality, guaranteed by a Republican form of Government. 

Why is the [[☃ definition 1]] denied us? Will any sane person declare that we are “Not of Sound mind”? . . . 

[H]ow extremely absurd it appears in the light of reason, to allow men the Elective Franchise who can neither read, nor write, and withhold it from women of cultivated minds and scholarly [[☃ definition 2]]. 

**Based on the excerpt, which amendment would Maria Chandler likely oppose?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Twelfth Amendment
The Fifteenth Amendment
The Seventeenth Amendment
The Nineteenth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “Address to the Women of Ohio,” a speech given at a women’s rights convention in Salem, Ohio in 1850.**

The legal theory is, marriage makes the husband and wife one person, and that person is the husband . . . Slaves are we, politically and legally. . . . If men would be worthy of the name, they must cease to [[☃ definition 1]] and rob their wives and mothers, they must [[☃ definition 2]] to [[☃ definition 3]] to political and legal slavery their sisters and daughters. And we women . . . must cease to submit to such tyranny.

**What concept does the author describe in the first sentence of the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: keep someone from having voting rights
put someone in a bad situation

choices.content: Coverture
Self-representation
Oligarchy
Indirect election

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “Address to the Women of Ohio,” a speech given at a women’s rights convention in Salem, Ohio in 1850.**

The legal theory is, marriage makes the husband and wife one person, and that person is the husband . . . Slaves are we, politically and legally. . . . If men would be worthy of the name, they must cease to [[☃ definition 1]] and rob their wives and mothers, they must [[☃ definition 2]] to [[☃ definition 3]] to political and legal slavery their sisters and daughters. And we women . . . must cease to submit to such tyranny.

**What change does the author call for in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: keep someone from having voting rights
put someone in a bad situation

choices.content: The election of women to Congress
The abolition of slavery
The legalization of divorce
The extension of suffrage to women

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Petition for Universal Suffrage (1866) written by Susan B. Anthony.**

To the Senate and House of Representatives:

The undersigned, Women of the United States, respectfully ask [for] an amendment of the Constitution that shall prohibit the several States from [[☃ definition 1]] any of their citizens on the ground of sex. In making our demand for Suffrage, we would call your attention to the fact that we represent fifteen million people—one half of the entire population of the country—intelligent, virtuous, native-born American citizens; and yet stand outside the [[☃ definition 2]]of political recognition.

The Constitution [[☃ definition 4]] us as "free people," and counts us whole persons in the basis of representation; and yet are we governed without our consent, [[☃ definition 3]] to pay taxes without appeal, and punished for violations of law without choice of judge or juror. As you are now amending the Constitution, and, in harmony with advancing civilization, placing new safeguards around the individual rights of four million of emancipated ex-slaves, we ask that you extend the right of Suffrage to Woman.

**What is Anthony’s main request to the government in this petition?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: depriving of the right to vote

choices.content: To give women the right to vote in elections
To remove legal barriers to voting like poll taxes
To make native-born women citizens of the United States
To abolish the institution of slavery across the United States

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Petition for Universal Suffrage (1866) written by Susan B. Anthony.**

To the Senate and House of Representatives:

The undersigned, Women of the United States, respectfully ask [for] an amendment of the Constitution that shall prohibit the several States from [[☃ definition 1]] any of their citizens on the ground of sex. In making our demand for Suffrage, we would call your attention to the fact that we represent fifteen million people—one half of the entire population of the country—intelligent, virtuous, native-born American citizens; and yet stand outside the [[☃ definition 2]] of political recognition.

The Constitution [[☃ definition 3]] us as "free people," and counts us whole persons in the basis of representation; and yet are we governed without our consent, [[☃ definition 4]] to pay taxes without appeal, and punished for violations of law without choice of judge or juror. As you are now amending the Constitution, and, in harmony with advancing civilization, placing new safeguards around the individual rights of four million of emancipated ex-slaves, we ask that you extend the right of Suffrage to Woman.

**In the last sentence of the petition, Anthony makes a specific request. Did Congress fulfill that request?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: depriving of the right to vote

choices.content: Yes, Congress extended citizenship to women and banned discrimination based on sex in the Fifteenth Amendment.
Yes, Congress passed a Voting Rights Act, giving states the right to choose whether to grant women the right to vote.
No, Congress banned women from voting in federal elections but allowed women to vote in state and local elections.
No, Congress ratified the Fourteenth and Fifteenth Amendments as written, limiting the right to vote to men.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**This political cartoon represents which strategy of the women’s suffrage movement?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A man wearing an outfit made to resemble the American flag (Uncle Sam) uses pruning shears to take care of a strange flower shaped like the United States. Several western states look like flowers, while most midwestern and eastern states are in black. There are four blank states that Uncle Sam prunes: Pennsylvania, New York, New Jersey, and Massachusetts. The flower is planted in soil labeled, “Liberty” and planted in a pot labeled “Equality.” In the background, there is a watering can labeled “Justice” and a pesticide pump sprayer (a machine used to kill bugs on plants) labeled “logic.” There is a caterpillar laying on top of the state of Maine, labeled “Anti.” There are larger shears labeled “Truth” and “Education.” Uncle Sam says “Prune away prejudice and these four States will blossom in November.”
options.caption: “Uncle Sam: Prune away Prejudice and these four States will blossom in November.” Blanche Ames, “The Map Blossoms,” *Woman’s Journal and Suffrage News,* 1915.

choices.content: The state-by-state push to change state laws and state constitutions
The effort to encourage states to ratify the Fourteenth and Fifteenth Amendments
The campaigns to elect more women to federal and state offices
The attempts by women to vote in federal elections across the states as an act of protest

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**The strategy displayed in the political cartoon led to which development?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A man wearing an outfit made to resemble the American flag (Uncle Sam) uses pruning shears to take care of a strange flower shaped like the United States. Several western states look like flowers, while most midwestern and eastern states are in black. There are four blank states that Uncle Sam prunes: Pennsylvania, New York, New Jersey, and Massachusetts. The flower is planted in soil labeled, “Liberty” and planted in a pot labeled “Equality.” In the background, there is a watering can labeled “Justice” and a pesticide pump sprayer (a machine used to kill bugs on plants) labeled “logic.” There is a caterpillar laying on top of the state of Maine, labeled “Anti.” There are larger shears labeled “Truth” and “Education.” Uncle Sam says “Prune away prejudice and these four States will blossom in November.”
options.caption: “Uncle Sam: Prune away Prejudice and these four States will blossom in November.” Blanche Ames, “The Map Blossoms,” *Woman’s Journal and Suffrage News,* 1915.

choices.content: The start of the women’s suffrage movement
The ratification of the Nineteenth Amendment
The passage of the Voting Rights Act
The addition of new western states to the United States

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from circuit justice Ward Hunt’s ruling in *United States v. Anthony* (1873).**

The inability of a state to [[☃ definition 3]] the right of voting on account of race, color, or previous condition of servitude, arises from a federal guarantee. Its violation would be the denial of a federal right—that is, a right belonging to the [[☃ definition 1]] as a citizen of the United States. This right, however, exists by virtue of the Fifteenth Amendment. If the Fifteenth Amendment had contained the word “sex,” the argument of the defendant would have been [[☃ definition 2]]. She would have said that an attempt by a state to deny the right to vote because one is of a particular sex is expressly prohibited by that amendment. The amendment, however, does not contain that word. It is limited to race, color, or previous condition of servitude. The legislature of the state of New York has seen fit to say that the franchise of voting shall be limited to the male sex. In saying this, there is, in my judgment, no violation of the letter, or of the spirit, of the Fourteenth or of the Fifteenth Amendment.

**Which statement best summarizes Justice Hunt’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Because the Fourteenth Amendment extends citizenship to women, they should have the same right to vote that men have.
Because the Fourteenth Amendment extended the federal government’s powers over elections, the federal government can force states to let women vote.
Because the Fifteenth Amendment protects against discrimination based on race, those protections extend to any form of discrimination, including by sex.
Because the Fifteenth Amendment does not specifically protect women from being denied the right to vote, states can limit the right to vote to only men.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from circuit justice Ward Hunt’s ruling in *United States v. Anthony* (1873).**

The inability of a state to [[☃ definition 1]] the right of voting on account of race, color, or previous condition of servitude, arises from a federal guarantee. Its violation would be the denial of a federal right—that is, a right belonging to the [[☃ definition 2]] as a citizen of the United States. This right, however, exists by virtue of the Fifteenth Amendment. If the Fifteenth Amendment had contained the word “sex,” the argument of the defendant would have been [[☃ definition 3]]. She would have said that an attempt by a state to deny the right to vote because one is of a particular sex is expressly prohibited by that amendment. The amendment, however, does not contain that word. It is limited to race, color, or previous condition of servitude. The legislature of the state of New York has seen fit to say that the franchise of voting shall be limited to the male sex. In saying this, there is, in my judgment, no violation of the letter, or of the spirit, of the Fourteenth or of the Fifteenth Amendment.

**The ruling in this case was eventually overturned by which development?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The meeting of the Seneca Falls Convention
The Supreme Court’s ruling in *Minor v. Hapersett*
The ratification of the Nineteenth Amendment
The ratification of the Equal Rights Amendment
